channel.title: Defense
channel.description: News, Analysis and Opinion from POLITICO

item.title: What Trump’s blowup with Rutte was really about
item.description: The president’s tirade against NATO chief Mark Rutte stemmed from his frustration that allies wouldn’t let the U.S. access military bases for attacks against Iran.

media:content.media:title: Secretary General Mark Rutte’s visit might actually have been well-timed.

item.dc:creator: By Eli Stokols, Paul McLeary and Jack Detsch
item.dc:contributor: By Eli Stokols, Paul McLeary and Jack Detsch

item.title: NATO chief insists Europe is ‘scrambling’ to help with Iran war after latest Trump attacks
item.description: The head of the alliance tried to thread the needle between an irate U.S. president and NATO members who feel unfairly maligned.

media:content.media:title: NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte speaks at the Ronald Reagan Institute on April 9, 2026, in Washington.

item.dc:creator: By Paul McLeary

item.title: ‘Seems like losing’: What the US hasn’t won in Iran
item.description: As negotiations begin this weekend in Pakistan, the war has reaffirmed Iran’s regional significance.

media:content.media:title: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth looks toward Joint Chiefs of Staff chair Gen. Dan Caine, as they speak to the media at the Pentagon on April 8, 2026.

item.dc:creator: By Paul McLeary and Jack Detsch
item.dc:contributor: By Paul McLeary and Jack Detsch

item.title: Hegseth declares victory in Iran but says US forces will remain in region
item.description: The Defense Secretary repeatedly referred to the U.S. military operation in the past tense.

media:content.media:title: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth speaks to members of the media during a press briefing at the Pentagon in Washington, Wednesday, April 8, 2026.

item.dc:creator: By Leo Shane III

item.title: Pentagon’s new plans in Iran give Trump a way out of war crime accusations
item.description: Defense officials are expanding a list of strike options.

media:content.media:title: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth speaks with reporters during a news conference with President Donald Trump and Joint Chiefs of Staff Chair Gen. Dan Caine at the White House on April 6, 2026.

item.dc:creator: By Paul McLeary and Leo Shane III
item.dc:contributor: By Paul McLeary and Leo Shane III

item.title: NATO’s Rutte to meet with Trump at White House amid attacks on alliance
item.description: The president has grown increasingly furious with the decades-old alliance amid the war in Iran.

media:content.media:title: NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte gives a press conference about NATO's general annual report in Brussels on March 26, 2026.

item.dc:creator: By Aaron Pellish

item.title: One pilot rescued after fighter jet crash in Iran
item.description: The second crew member’s fate is unknown.

media:content.media:title: An F-15 Eagle fighter aircraft flies on Sept. 24, 2024.

item.dc:creator: By Jack Detsch and Paul McLeary
item.dc:contributor: By Jack Detsch and Paul McLeary

item.title: Hegseth ousts top Army general
item.description: The departure of Army Chief of State Gen. Randy George follows a string of high-level firings under Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and comes amid the Iran war.

media:content.media:title: Randy George is the latest in a line of more than a dozen top military officials that Pete Hegseth has ousted.

item.dc:creator: By Jack Detsch

item.title: Iran war puts Cuba on the back burner
item.description: The administration has a lot to deal with in the Middle East – and doesn’t want to hassle with Russia.

media:content.media:title: A man walks as the Russian oil tanker Anatoly Kolodkin is seen at the oil terminal in the port of Matanzas, northwestern Cuba, on March 31, 2026.

item.dc:creator: By Giselle Ruhiyyih Ewing and Eric Bazail-Eimil
item.dc:contributor: By Giselle Ruhiyyih Ewing and Eric Bazail-Eimil

item.title: Officials warn US is running out of targets to strike in Iran
item.description: Iran is fighting a different kind of war, one less focused on military tactics than economics.

media:content.media:title: Smoke rises from the site of a strike in Tehran, Iran on April 1, 2026.

item.dc:creator: By Leo Shane III, Jack Detsch and Connor O'Brien
item.dc:contributor: By Leo Shane III, Jack Detsch and Connor O'Brien

item.title: NASA's historic moon mission blasts into space
item.description: The high-stakes journey will slingshot four astronauts around the moon and back.

media:content.media:title: The Artemis II crewed lunar mission launches at Kennedy Space Center in Cape Canaveral, Florida, on April 1, 2026. Artemis II is a 10-day flight with four astronauts on a loop around the Moon, marking the first time humans have traveled beyond low Earth orbit since the Apollo 17 mission in 1972.

item.dc:creator: By Audrey Decker

item.title: Trump has threatened to leave NATO over Iran. There are few signs that’s happening.
item.description: A withdrawal from the alliance would require discussions with lawmakers and a defined process that does not appear to have started.

media:content.media:title: President Donald Trump meets with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte in the Oval Office on October 22, 2025.

item.dc:creator: By Jack Detsch, Connor O'Brien and Victor Jack
item.dc:contributor: By Jack Detsch, Connor O'Brien and Victor Jack

item.title: Isaacman aims to reinvigorate NASA’s image, starting with the moon
item.description: The space agency is preparing to launch its highest-stakes mission in decades, carrying astronauts deeper into space than ever before.

media:content.media:credit: Miguel J. Rodriguez Carrillo/AFP via Getty Images
media:content.media:title: The Space Launch System (SLS) rocket and the Orion spacecraft are seen at Launch Pad 39B at the Kennedy Space Center in Cape Canaveral, Florida, Feb. 1, 2026.

item.dc:creator: By Audrey Decker

item.title: Trump says he expects US to leave Iran in ‘2 or 3 weeks’
item.description: The president will address the nation Wednesday evening on the war.

media:content.media:title: US President Donald Trump speaks after signing an executive order in the Oval Office of the White House in Washington, DC, on March 31, 2026.

item.dc:creator: By Leo Shane III

item.title: US officials tell allies Iran war could delay Ukraine weapons shipments
item.description: The warning comes as the U.S. burns through air defense interceptors and other weapons in its military campaign against Tehran.

media:content.media:title: Secretary of State Marco Rubio arrives to attend talks during a G7 Foreign Ministers' meeting outside Paris on March 27, 2026.

item.dc:creator: By Jack Detsch and Victor Jack
item.dc:contributor: By Jack Detsch and Victor Jack

item.title: ‘I have no idea what they are trying to do’: Allies say Trump sends mixed signals on Iran
item.description: The administration’s mixed messaging has spooked U.S. partners and allies fearful of worsening economic fallout if the president intensifies the war on Iran

media:content.media:title: While President Donald Trump has spoken frequently about his plans for Iran, some allies are beginning to tune him out.

item.dc:creator: By Felicia Schwartz, Phelim Kine and Nahal Toosi
item.dc:contributor: By Felicia Schwartz, Phelim Kine and Nahal Toosi

item.title: Allies and Congress are about to lose a key window into US military plans
item.description: The Pentagon’s decision not to release a Global Posture Review follows the go-it-alone pattern of the Trump administration.

media:content.media:credit: Middle East Images/AFP via Getty Images
media:content.media:title: Americans celebrate the Army's 250th anniversary with a parade on the National Mall in Washington D.C., Saturday, June 14, 2025.

item.dc:creator: By Paul McLeary, Leo Shane III and Chris Lunday
item.dc:contributor: By Paul McLeary, Leo Shane III and Chris Lunday

item.title: Top Republican attacks Pentagon for not providing details on Iran
item.description: The House Armed Services Committee chair said the department owes lawmakers more information about the administration’s plans for U.S. troops in the Middle East.

media:content.media:title: "That's what I conveyed to them at the end of this hearing, is this has consequences if you don't remedy it," Rep. Mike Rogers told reporters.

item.dc:creator: By Connor O'Brien

item.title: Republicans reject limits on Iran war, despite deepening conflict
item.description: This was the third test of GOP support for the president's actions, even as oil prices have shot up and the American public appears increasingly disenchanted with the war.

media:content.media:title: Democrats, as part of their push, want Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth (right) and Secretary of State Marco Rubio to agree to testify publicly on Iran.

item.dc:creator: By Connor O'Brien

item.title: Pentagon prepares to send another 3,000 troops to Middle East
item.description: The decision to move the Army’s 82nd Airborne Division to the region heightens the possibility U.S. troops will enter Iran.

media:content.media:title: Domestically built Iranian missiles are displayed as part of a permanent exhibition in Tehran, Iran on March 24, 2026.

item.dc:creator: By Paul McLeary

item.title: Pentagon weighs sending more troops to Middle East
item.description: The discussions, while evolving, would mark a drastic new step in the Iran war.

media:content.media:title: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth speaks during a press briefing at the Pentagon on March 19, 2026.

item.dc:creator: By Paul McLeary

item.title: Congress braces for $200B Iran war request
item.description: The ballooning cost of the war is certain to intensify an already toxic fight on Capitol Hill over the Trump administration's three-week military campaign in the Middle East.

media:content.media:title: "It takes money to kill bad guys,” Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said at a Pentagon briefing Thursday.

item.dc:creator: By Connor O'Brien and Leo Shane III
item.dc:contributor: By Connor O'Brien and Leo Shane III

item.title: Senate rejects limits on Trump as Iran war intensifies
item.description: Democrats have filed a half dozen war powers resolutions aimed at keeping the pressure on Republicans amid Trump's ongoing campaign against Iran.

media:content.media:title: Sen. Cory Booker, D-N.J., right, is joined by Sen. Tim Kaine, D-Va., left, for a press briefing on the Iran war powers resolution at the Capitol, Monday, March 9, 2026, in Washington.

item.dc:creator: By Connor O'Brien

item.title: Inside the White House plan to sell the Iran war online
item.description: The administration is bucking decades of decorum by depicting the Iran war as a violent video game.

media:content.media:credit: Illustration by Claudine Hellmuth/POLITICO (source images via Getty and White House)
media:content.media:title: Main.WH-Pentagon-Comms.jpg

item.dc:creator: By Eli Stokols, Ben Johansen, Jack Detsch and Paul McLeary
item.dc:contributor: By Eli Stokols, Ben Johansen, Jack Detsch and Paul McLeary

item.title: Estonia ‘ready to talk’ on Strait of Hormuz backup
item.description: Although Defense Minister Hanno Pevkur said there had not been an official U.S. request to Tallinn for help, he pointed to President Donald Trump’s broader call for NATO allies to provide assistance.

media:content.media:title: Estonia's Defence Minister Hanno Pevkur looks on at the NATO headquarters in Brussels on Oct. 15, 2025.

item.dc:creator: By Daniella Cheslow

item.title: Pentagon says lethal boat strikes are ‘just the beginning’ in South, Central America
item.description: A top DOD official also told lawmakers on Tuesday that the possibility of deploying ground forces hasn’t been ruled out.

media:content.media:title: The Pentagon is seen from an airplane on Feb. 2 in Washington.

item.dc:creator: By Leo Shane III

item.title: ‘Never heard him so angry’: Trump is furious that global allies aren’t pitching in in Iran
item.description: Their assistance, the president said on Tuesday, is no longer needed.

media:content.media:title: President Donald Trump has repeatedly asked global allies — and some geopolitical foes, including China — for help securing the Strait of Hormuz.

item.dc:creator: By Gregory Svirnovskiy and Jacob Wendler
item.dc:contributor: By Gregory Svirnovskiy and Jacob Wendler

item.title: Crew of fatal US military crash included Alabama father recently deployed and several from Ohio
item.description: The aircraft was supporting military operations against Iran.

media:content.media:credit: Paul Mann/U.S. Air National Guard via AP
media:content.media:title: U.S. Air Force Capt. Alex Klinner, operations flight commander for the 99th Air Refueling Squadron, completes a lap at Sumpter Smith Joint National Guard Base, Birmingham, Alabama, on May 2, 2025.

item.dc:creator: By Associated Press

item.title: Hegseth ignored military officials when he slashed offices that limit risk to civilians
item.description: The Defense secretary’s decision to cut offices that mitigate civilian harm faces renewed attention as the Pentagon investigates a strike that killed hundreds of Iranian children.

media:content.media:credit: Sepehr-M /Middle East Images / AFP via Getty Images
media:content.media:title: Smoke and fire rise amid airstrikes at the Mehrabad International Airport in Tehran on March 7, 2026.

item.dc:creator: By Jack Detsch and Paul McLeary
item.dc:contributor: By Jack Detsch and Paul McLeary

item.title: Hegseth, attacking press, declares victory is near amid ongoing Iran conflict
item.description: The Defense secretary gave little indication on how or when the war would end.

media:content.media:title: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth speaks at the inaugural Americas Counter Cartel Conference at U.S. Southern Command in Doral, Florida, on March 5, 2026.

item.dc:creator: By Paul McLeary
